definition: An unforeseen, unintended event; something unexpected.

One of the requirements of an insurable risk — losses must be unintentional from the insured's standpoint. Solution: deductibles and claim investigations encourage the insured to avoid losses and discourage fraud.

The Association for Cooperative Operations Research and Development — an industry organization that develops standardized insurance forms. ACORD 25 (Certificate of Insurance) and ACORD 125 (Commercial Insurance Application) are among the most widely used forms in the industry.
An event arising out of natural causes with no human intervention which could not have been prevented by reasonable care or foresight. Examples are floods, lightning, and earthquakes.

Property valuation that equals replacement cost minus depreciation. Pays "what the property was worth at the time of loss," not what it costs to replace. Lower premium, but may leave the insured under-indemnified.
A formal document prepared by an actuary that documents methods, assumptions, and conclusions used in setting reserves, rates, or other financial measurements.
A person who compiles and analyzes statistics to calculate risk and determine insurance rates and premiums.
A person or entity added to another's insurance policy, receiving coverage for certain liability exposures. Common in contractor-subcontractor, lessor-lessee, and vendor relationships.
Extra charges covered by homeowner's policies over and above the policyholder's customary living expenses due to damage by a covered peril that makes the home temporarily uninhabitable.
Characteristic of an insurance contract. Means that one party (the insurer) sets the terms, and the other party (the policyholder) can "take it or leave it."
A person who investigates and settles insurance claims.
An insurance company licensed by the state to sell specific lines of insurance within that state. Policies issued by admitted insurers are backed by the state's guaranty fund.

An insurance company licensed by a state's insurance department to sell insurance in that state. Admitted carriers are backed by the state guaranty fund, which protects policyholders if the carrier becomes insolvent. Also called "Authorized Carrier."
Premiums are typically tax deductible in the year they are paid; transfers the risk of catastrophic loss to an insurer; administrative services included.
Avoids premium taxes, insurer administrative expenses, and marketing costs. Retains investment income on loss reserves.

The tendency for people or entities with a higher-than-average chance of loss to seek insurance at standard rates. When only high-risk individuals buy a policy, costs rise and the pool becomes unprofitable.
A billing arrangement where the agency bills the policyholder, collects the premium, deducts commission, and remits the balance to the carrier. More common for commercial accounts.

A valued policy in which the insurer and the insured agree to a specific value for an item, appraised at the inception of the policy. Often used to insure items whose value is difficult to quantify, such as antiques or fine art. Also called a Guaranteed Value policy.
A property coverage option where the insurer and insured agree in advance to a specific value, waiving coinsurance. In the event of a total loss, the agreed amount is paid regardless of actual value.
One of the four requirements of a legally binding contract. All parties involved must agree to the terms of the contract. Can also refer to a binder, which is the preliminary substance of a contract.
An entry-level designation covering fundamentals of insurance operations, personal lines, and commercial lines. Popular for newer insurance professionals.

Coverage for the insured in the event that the insured's negligent acts and/or omissions result in losses in connection with the use, ownership, or maintenance of aircraft. Includes aircraft hull (physical damage) and aviation liability to passengers, airports, and other third parties.

A characteristic of an insurance contract. Means "depending on an unknown future event." An insurance contract will only pay IF and WHEN covered damages occur. Neither party knows how much the contract will end up paying when they enter into the contract.
The term "All-Risk Insurance" is used to mean insurance against loss of or damage to property arising from any cause except those that are specifically excluded.

A financial strength rating assigned to insurance companies by AM Best Company. Ratings range from A++ (Superior) to D (Poor), with separate categories for companies under regulatory supervision. Agencies and commercial clients often require carriers to carry a minimum A rating (e.g., "A-" or better, Class VII or higher).
The person during whose life an annuity is payable — usually, but not always, the person who purchased the annuity. The annuitant receives the annuity payments.

In liability cases, the defendant's response to a complaint. There are three possible answers: (1) Accept complaint and pay for damages, (2) Deny the complaint, (3) Accept the complaint with a right to insert evidence into the case.

A formal authorization by an insurance company granting a licensed producer the right to sell that carrier's products. An agent may be licensed but not "appointed" by a specific carrier until that carrier appoints them.

The process in which there is a dispute between the insurer and the insured regarding the amount of the claim. Both parties hire an appraiser to represent them. The two appraisers attempt to settle the dispute. If they can not settle the dispute they hire an umpire to settle it.
A designation from The Institutes for professionals who want expertise in identifying, analyzing, and treating risk. Widely held by risk managers, brokers, and loss control consultants.

Security measures and practices to deter arson losses — lighting, fencing, access control, security cameras, alarm monitoring, and housekeeping. Arson is often the leading cause of large commercial property losses in vacant buildings.
A state pool that writes auto insurance for drivers who have been declined by the voluntary market. Often called the residual market. Premiums are higher and coverage may be limited.
A state-run plan that writes insurance for individuals and businesses that have been declined in the regular (voluntary) market. Common in auto insurance for drivers with poor records.

A state-administered pool that provides workers compensation insurance to employers who cannot obtain it in the voluntary market. In most states, administered by NCCI; some states run their own pools or state funds.

Land motor vehicle, trailer, or semitrailer including any attached machinery or equipment: (1) designed for travel on public roads, (2) subject to compulsory or financial responsibility laws or other motor vehicle laws. Does NOT include mobile equipment.
A person or business having temporary possession of property belonging to someone else — a dry cleaner holding clothes, a parking garage holding cars, a warehouse storing goods.

term: Balance Sheet in Loss Exposure Analysis
definition: Lists assets and liabilities of an organization, providing insight into its financial health and the value of property exposures. Used to identify what could be lost.

term: BASICs (Behavior Analysis and Safety Improvement Categories)
definition: The seven safety categories tracked under CSA: Unsafe Driving, Hours of Service Compliance, Driver Fitness, Controlled Substances/Alcohol, Vehicle Maintenance, Hazardous Materials Compliance, and Crash Indicator.

A safety program that focuses on observable worker behavior — observing safe and at-risk behaviors, providing feedback, and reinforcing safe practices. Proven to reduce injuries but requires active management involvement.
Person or group that receives benefits, profits, or advantages.

term: Benefits of Risk Management for Brokers
definition: Increased customer retention and referrals; increased claim satisfaction; reduced E&O (errors & omissions) potential; and better-informed clients who make sounder coverage decisions.
A surety bond guaranteeing that a contractor will enter into a contract if it is awarded to him, and will furnish any performance bond required by the contract.

A temporary contract that provides coverage until the formal policy can be issued. May be oral or written. Typically limited to 30-90 days. The insurer has the right to cancel during this period if the risk proves unacceptable.

The authority granted by an insurance company to an agent to bind coverage on the company's behalf — creating immediate coverage before the policy is formally issued. Subject to underwriting guidelines.
Similar to binder authority — the agent can commit the company to coverage. Often limited by line of business, limits, and specific risk characteristics.

A single limit of insurance applying collectively to multiple locations or categories of property. Provides flexibility if values are higher at one location than originally reported. May include a margin clause.
Provides coverage for non-trucking use when the truck-tractor is not being used to haul or carry a trailer for others.
Physical injury to a person.

Covers losses resulting from the malfunction of boilers, machinery, and electrical equipment. Benefits include: (1) property of the insured directly damaged by the accident; (2) costs of temporary repairs and expediting expenses; and (3) liability for damage to the property of others. Also called Equipment Breakdown.
The collection of insurance policies an agent, agency, or carrier currently has in force. An agency's book is its primary asset; often valued at 1.5x to 3x annual commissions in agency sales.

A visual risk-assessment method showing causes of a risk event on the left (leading to the event in the center) and consequences on the right. Controls are placed on both sides — preventive controls to stop the event, mitigative controls to reduce consequences.
The failure or falsehood of a stated promise of a product stipulation.
A licensed person or organization paid by a client to look for insurance on the client's behalf. A broker represents the buyer; an agent typically represents the insurer.
A $75,000 surety bond required by FMCSA for property freight brokers to ensure they can pay motor carriers for services rendered. Alternative is a BMC-85 trust fund.
Cutting a felled tree into logs of specified lengths. Hazards include chainsaw contact, binding/spring back, and unstable logs.

Typically carried by contractors or property owners to cover damage to a construction project while it is in progress. Covers the structure, materials on site, and materials in transit. Ends when the project is complete and occupied.
Coverage against loss as a result of forced entry into premises. Different from theft — burglary requires evidence of forced entry.

Used to insure the private passenger and commercial auto exposures of all businesses other than garages (auto dealers, auto service businesses, etc.), truckers, and motor carriers (trucks hauling cargo). Includes: liability coverage; physical damage coverage (comprehensive, specified causes of loss, and collision). Uninsured motorists, medical payments, and underinsured motorists coverage can be added by endorsement.

Excluded: expected or intended injuries; liability assumed under a contract (except insured contracts); work-related injuries to an employee; damage to property owned, transported, or in the care/custody/control of the insured; BI or PD from operation of self-propelled vehicles with attached cherry pickers used to raise or lower workers, or permanently attached equipment (air compressors, pumps, generators); pollution damage (except as provided); covered autos while being used for organized or professional racing, demolition, or stunting.
A written plan for maintaining or rapidly resuming critical operations during and after a disruption. Includes people, processes, technology, facilities, and communications.

Coverage for lost net income and continuing expenses when a covered property loss suspends operations. Pays until business operations are restored, subject to a waiting period (typically 72 hours) and a period of indemnity.
The termination of insurance coverage during the policy period. Flat cancellation is cancellation as of the effective date with no premium charge.
Mid-term termination of a policy by the insurer for specific reasons permitted by state law — nonpayment of premium, material misrepresentation, or substantial increase in hazard.

When a policy is terminated midterm by the insurance company, the earned premium is calculated only for the days coverage was provided. Example: annual $1,000 policy canceled after 40 days → earned premium = 40/365 × $1,000 = $110.

When the policy is terminated prior to expiration at the policyholder's request. The earned premium charged is higher than pro-rata (typically the return premium is about 90% of the pro-rata refund) to cover the insurer's acquisition costs.
An insurance company established by a parent firm (or group of firms) for the purpose of insuring the parent's exposures. A way for large organizations to self-insure in an organized structure.
Coverage for goods being transported by a motor carrier while in the carrier's care, custody, or control. Required by FMCSA for certain commodities; often contractually required by shippers.
A large-magnitude loss that is difficult to forecast and often affects many insureds simultaneously — hurricanes, earthquakes, wildfires. May be subject to separate limits or deductibles.

A computer-based process that simulates thousands of plausible catastrophic events using statistical, financial, engineering, and scientific data plus policy coverage information to estimate aggregate financial loss, including insured loss.
Health insurance that provides protection against the high cost of treating severe or lengthy illness or disabilities and usually has a high deductible and lower premiums.
Accidents where a person is pinned or crushed between two objects — between a log and equipment, between moving equipment, or in a rollover. Second-leading cause of logging fatalities.
A form included in a commercial package policy or a commercial property policy that lists the causes of loss against which the insured property is covered.
Similar to OCIP but sponsored and controlled by the general contractor rather than the owner. Covers the GC and participating subcontractors for a project.
A service where fire and burglar alarms are transmitted to a UL-certified or FM-approved monitoring center that dispatches emergency services. Qualifies the property for insurance credits.

An official document that contains the details of an insurance policy. It typically lists the insurance company, the named insured, what is covered, and the dates of the policy period. Policyholders may be required to present their certificate as proof that insurance coverage is in effect.
A high-energy failure mode when a cable or chain breaks under load — fragments can be ejected at bullet-like velocities. A leading cause of severe injuries and fatalities in cable yarding operations.
Core controls for chainsaw operations: chaps, boots, hard hat with visor, hearing protection, kickback prevention, proper felling techniques, refueling away from ignition sources.
Children's Health Insurance Program. A program that provides low-cost health coverage to children in families who earn too much money to qualify for Medicaid.

A practical professional designation earned by completing 5 week-long courses covering personal lines, commercial casualty, commercial property, life/health, and agency management. Popular among producers and account managers.
A designation focused on insurance coverage knowledge and client service skills, most often held by customer service representatives and account managers.
Pays lost business income when access to the insured's premises is prohibited by civil authorities (e.g., police closure, evacuation orders) due to a covered cause of loss at a nearby property.
Observes court cases of one citizen charging another citizen for damages caused by tort. Differs from criminal law.
A formal request from the customer to an insurance company asking for a payment based on the terms of the insurance policy.
A type of liability insurance form that only pays if BOTH the event that causes the claim AND the actual claim are reported to the insurer during the policy term. Contrasts with an "occurrence" form.
A fire involving ordinary combustibles — wood, paper, cloth, plastics. Water or multi-purpose extinguishers are appropriate.
A fire involving flammable liquids or gases — gasoline, oil, propane. Water is ineffective or dangerous; use foam, dry chemical, or CO2 extinguishers.
A fire involving energized electrical equipment. Water is dangerous; use non-conductive extinguishing agents.
A fire involving combustible metals — magnesium, titanium, sodium. Requires specialized Class D extinguishing agents.
A fire involving cooking oils and fats. Found in commercial kitchens. Requires wet chemical (Class K) extinguishers.

A numeric code assigned to a business based on the type of work it does, used to determine workers compensation premium rates. Each class has a loss cost per $100 of payroll. For example, NCCI class 2710 covers logging operations.
Classified by: (1) type of exposed values, (2) perils involved, (3) financial consequences.

A property insurance provision that requires the policyholder to carry adequate coverage (typically at least 80% of the property's value). If a property does not meet this requirement (i.e., it is underinsured), the insurer applies a penalty on claims for partial loss. The penalty requires the policyholder to pay a percentage of the claim, depending on how underinsured the property is. To calculate: divide the actual coverage by the coinsurance requirement and then multiply that by the loss. In health insurance, coinsurance refers to the portion of a covered claim that the insured is responsible for paying out-of-pocket, after the deductible has been paid.
(Had / Should) × Partial Loss — where "Had" is the actual coverage and "Should" is the coinsurance requirement.
Auto insurance coverage that will reimburse the insured for damages sustained to their personal automobile that is due to the fault of the insured driver.
Auto insurance that protects you against costs to repair your own vehicle after a crash.
An auto coverage that waives your collision deductible if you are hit by a negligent uninsured motorist.

An indicator of an insurance company's underwriting profitability. Combined Ratio = Loss Ratio + Expense Ratio. Under 100% means the insurer made an underwriting profit; over 100% means an underwriting loss (though investment income may offset this).

Covers damage to a business's physical assets — buildings, office equipment, furniture, inventory, and materials — caused by covered perils such as fire, theft, or vandalism. Typically written on a replacement-cost or actual-cash-value basis.
The percentage of premium paid by an insurance company to an agent or agency for placing and servicing business. Varies by line (higher for new commercial; lower for personal auto renewals).
Based on court decisions and customs when statutory law does not provide an answer; creates a precedent.
This rule means that if the plaintiff was at all responsible for his own loss, he will receive a modified judgement, depending on the extent of his own negligence.
Money awarded in a civil court for tangible and intangible damages caused by the policyholder. There are two types: general and special.
Money awarded for the emotional losses of the plaintiff that will continue after the trial date. These are subjective values and are determined by the court.
Money awarded for the exact value of the physical damage caused to the plaintiff up to the trial date. This is an objective value determined by receipts and medical bills.
One of the four qualifications of a legally binding contract. All parties must be competent, with the necessary legal and mental capacity.
Initiates a civil lawsuit by one person (the plaintiff) requesting financial relief from damages caused by someone else (the defendant).

A more comprehensive FMCSA audit triggered by poor SMS scores, crashes, or complaints. May result in a safety rating (Satisfactory, Conditional, or Unsatisfactory). Unsatisfactory carriers may be placed out of service.
Covering or providing broad protection against loss.
Auto insurance that protects you against costs to repair or replace your vehicle after events out of your control such as weather, vandalism, theft, etc.
Coverage on an "all-risks" basis for glass breakage, subject to exclusions of war and fire.
The act of withholding relevant material facts from an insurer.

A property loss caused by two or more perils occurring at nearly the same time, where at least one is covered and at least one is excluded. Can create disputes — whether the loss is covered depends on policy language and state law.
A binder effective only upon certain conditions — such as the insured meeting specified loss control requirements, providing additional information, or installing required safety systems.

A temporary agreement issued when an insurance application and first premium are taken, providing limited coverage while the application is underwritten. If the applicant proves insurable as applied for, coverage applies retroactively from the receipt date.
The section of the policy that qualifies or limits the insurer's promise to pay or perform.
The portion of a car insurance policy that spells out the details for how to make a claim, what information you must have, how much time you have, and how to cancel your policy.

A space large enough to enter to work but not designed for continuous occupancy, with limited entry/exit (tanks, silos, manholes, pits). OSHA Permit-Required Confined Space standards impose entry procedures, atmospheric testing, and rescue plans.
One of the four qualifications of a legally binding contract. All parties must bring something of value to the contract.

Categories used in property underwriting based on building materials: Frame (wood), Joisted Masonry, Non-Combustible (steel), Masonry Non-Combustible, Modified Fire Resistive, and Fire Resistive (concrete/steel). Better construction = lower rates.
Additional compensation paid by a carrier to an agency based on the profitability and/or volume of business placed. Typically calculated on a calendar-year basis.
State-level requirement that insurance licensees periodically complete a minimum number of hours of insurance-related education in order to be eligible for license renewal.
An agreement entered into voluntarily by two parties or more with the intention of creating a legal obligation.
An area of law regulating how to make and enforce agreements between people.

An inland marine coverage for mobile equipment owned or leased by contractors — excavators, loaders, generators, welders. Covers loss anywhere within the policy territory, whether at a job site, in transit, or in storage.
Pollution liability coverage specific to contracting operations. Covers pollution conditions arising from the insured's operations at a job site.
Shifting legal financial responsibility for a loss to another party through a contract — such as hold-harmless agreements, leases, or insurance contracts.
According to this rule, if the plaintiff was in any way responsible for his own loss, he will not be entitled to any indemnification.
A process in which business units evaluate their own controls against identified risks. Produces a consistent view across the organization and engages front-line managers in risk awareness.

A provision used when a person is covered by more than one health insurance plan. COB determines which plan pays first (primary) and which pays second (secondary) to prevent over-insurance and ensure orderly claims payment.
A fixed dollar amount that you agree to pay each time you receive medical treatment, such as a doctor's visit or prescription.

The four fundamental underwriting factors for commercial property risk. Construction: building materials and fire resistance. Occupancy: what happens inside (office vs. woodworking). Protection: fire protection systems and public fire services. Exposure: neighboring property that could cause or be damaged by loss.

A risk-management framework developed by the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations. More compliance-oriented; focused on maximizing owners' and shareholders' equity. Defines risk as having an adverse effect on objectives.

COSO is more compliance-oriented and maximizes owner/shareholder equity, treating risk as adverse. ISO 31000 is more proactive, applies to a broader range of organizations, and treats risk as the effect of uncertainty on objectives (positive or negative).

The signature of a licensed resident agent on an insurance policy issued for risks in that state. Historically required in many states to ensure local agency involvement; less common today but still applies in some jurisdictions.
Total amount and type of insurance carried.

The specific policy document used to provide a type of insurance coverage — such as the Commercial General Liability form (CG 00 01) or the Business Auto form (CA 00 01). Each form has its own insuring agreement, exclusions, and conditions.
The maximum amount an insurance company will pay if you file a claim.
The amount and extent of protection provided by an insurance policy.

A professional designation awarded by The Institutes to those who complete an 8-course curriculum covering risk management, insurance operations, underwriting, claims, commercial lines, and finance. Requires 3+ years of insurance experience and an ethics commitment.
Insurance issued to a creditor (lender) to cover the life of a debtor (borrower) for an outstanding loan. If the debtor dies, the balance is paid off.

Coverage for loss from crimes committed against the insured — employee dishonesty, forgery, computer fraud, funds transfer fraud, robbery, safe burglary. Commonly bundled as a Commercial Crime Policy.

A plan for responding to low-probability, high-impact events that threaten the organization's viability or reputation — active shooter, product contamination, executive scandal. Focuses on decision-making and communication.
A designation earned by completing 5 courses on risk assessment, analysis, control, financing, and practice. The risk-management counterpart to the CIC designation.

A policy provision that treats each insured under the policy as if they had separate coverage for claims made by one insured against another. Allows additional insureds and named insureds to sue each other without coverage conflicts.
FMCSA's program that measures motor carrier safety performance. Uses seven BASIC categories and produces a carrier score used for enforcement prioritization.
Coverage for cyber commerce exposures including data breaches, copyright infringement, libel, and violation of privacy. Increasingly essential for any business handling customer data.

Coverage for privacy breaches, data security incidents, business interruption from cyberattacks, cyber extortion, and media liability. Typically includes first-party (own damages) and third-party (claims by others) coverages.
Intangible losses, such as pain and suffering or mental anguish.
Tangible, financial losses that can be documented.
A standing tree with defects that make it likely to fail — dead, diseased, leaning, or damaged. Must be assessed and often removed before any work near it.
States that anyone involved in the use of inherently dangerous products or machines is 100% liable for their own damages.

term: DART Rate (Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred)
definition: An OSHA metric measuring the rate of injuries causing days away from work, restricted activity, or job transfer per 100 full-time workers per year. Formula: (DART cases × 200,000) ÷ total hours worked.

The declaration is the part of the written insurance policy that states all the policy's specifics, including the name of the policyholder, the type of property insured, the premiums, and the term limits for the coverage.
An insurer's refusal to write a risk. Documenting declinations from admitted carriers is typically required before surplus lines coverage can be placed.

A record of the insurers that have refused to write a risk. Required documentation for surplus lines placements in most states — typically 3-5 declinations from admitted carriers must be proven before surplus lines coverage can be bound.
Takes place when an insurance company rejects an application for coverage.
The amount the insured must pay in a loss before any payment is due from the company.
One specific, predetermined amount that a policyholder must pay out-of-pocket before his policy coverage kicks in.

The policyholder only pays if the total damages come out to less than his deductible. If the cost of damages equals or exceeds the deductible, the insurer pays the full amount and the policyholder pays nothing.
A deductible that is calculated as a percentage of the value of the insured risk.

A judgement entered by the court against a party who fails to present his case or defense in a civil action. Most commonly, this applies when the defendant fails to show up and answer the claim brought against him, so the court decides in favor of the plaintiff.
Page in policy that gives specified limited meaning to terms used in the policy.
The act of lowering an item's value due to use or wear and tear. Depreciation is based on Age, Condition, and Life Expectancy.

The total decrease of an item's value over a period of time. Formula: annual depreciation × number of years used. Subtract this number from the item's replacement cost to get its actual cash value (ACV).
An item's replacement cost divided by the number of years in its expected lifespan.
One of the requirements of an insurable risk — the loss must be definite in time, place, cause, and amount. Emotional distress, for example, is harder to assign a precise dollar value.
A billing arrangement where the insurance company bills the policyholder directly. The agent receives a commission check from the carrier.
An insurance company that sells policies to the insured through salaried representatives or exclusive agents only — not through independent brokers.
Liability coverage protecting directors and officers of a corporation against claims arising out of the performance of their professional duties on behalf of the corporation.
A type of insurance paid to an individual if they are injured and unable to work for a specified length of time.
One large catastrophic loss could deplete reserves and potentially wipe out the organization. Often paired with stop-loss insurance.
A subset of a business continuity plan focused on restoring IT systems and data after a disruption. Includes backup procedures, recovery time objectives (RTO), and recovery point objectives (RPO).
A refund of a portion of the premium paid by the insured from insurer surplus, typically paid by mutual insurance companies when claim experience and investment returns are favorable.
The contract must provide the coverage that a policyholder might reasonably expect, despite the actual policy language.
A unique identifier assigned by FMCSA to commercial motor vehicles operating in interstate commerce. Required on the vehicle and used to track safety records.

Extends business auto coverage to non-owned vehicles operated by the person (or spouse) named in the endorsement. Coverage does not apply to autos owned by the person named in the endorsement or spouse. Also called "Broadened Coverage for Named Individuals."

A DOT-required file maintained for each commercial driver, containing their employment application, MVR, road test certificate, medical examiner's certificate, annual review, and other required documents.
FMCSA requires commercial drivers to be tested pre-employment, post-accident, randomly, reasonably suspicious, return-to-duty, and follow-up. Results go into the FMCSA Clearinghouse.
The portion of premium for which the insurance company has already provided coverage. If a 12-month policy has been in force for 6 months, 50% of the premium has been earned.

Generally excluded from standard property policies; purchased as a separate policy or endorsement. Usually has a significant deductible (often a percentage of the insured value rather than a dollar amount).
The date on which an insurance policy or bond goes into effect — and from which protection is furnished.
FMCSA-mandated electronic device that records driver hours of service. Replaced paper logs for most commercial drivers as of December 2019.
Liability coverage for bodily injury or property damage arising from the use of elevators or escalators operated, maintained, or controlled by the insured.

A documented plan describing actions to be taken in the event of a natural disaster, emergency evacuation, terrorist attack, or any incident that disrupts normal operations. Tells employees what to do and who is responsible.
Liability protection for an employer for claims arising from errors in administration of employee benefit plans — pension plans, group life, group health, group disability, and AD&D.
Coverage for loss of money, securities, or property caused by dishonest acts of employees. A component of most commercial crime policies.
Liability insurance for employers providing coverage for wrongful termination, discrimination, sexual harassment, or retaliation claims from current or former employees.

The workers compensation experience modifier. A number that adjusts premium based on a business's claim history versus industry peers. Below 1.00 = better than average (discount); above 1.00 = worse than average (surcharge). Critical to many commercial contracts that require a Mod below 1.0.
An endorsement is a specific addition to a policy that alters the coverage and therefore the price of that policy. Endorsements can either add or remove specific types of coverages.
An integrated approach that sees risk as an organization-wide issue to be managed across divisions and functions — not in silos. Frameworks include COSO ERM and ISO 31000.
Site-specific pollution coverage for owned or operated premises — covers gradual and sudden pollution conditions, including unknown pre-existing conditions in some forms.
Liability coverage for bodily injury or property damage arising from acids, fumes, smoke, toxic chemicals, waste materials, or other pollutants.

Coverage for loss or damage to equipment from mechanical or electrical breakdown. Covers the equipment itself plus resulting business income and extra expense. Essential for HVAC, production equipment, and electrical systems.

The science of fitting the job to the worker to prevent musculoskeletal injuries from repetitive motion, awkward postures, heavy lifting, or vibration. A major focus of loss control in manual-intensive industries.

Professional liability coverage for insurance agents and brokers against claims of negligence or errors in their services — failing to procure requested coverage, misquoting, errors in policy servicing, etc. Essential coverage for any insurance agency.

A legal principle that bars a party from asserting something contrary to what has been implied by its previous actions or statements. This is a form of "implied waiver"; for example, if the insurer regularly accepts late payments, it waives its right to deny coverage due to late payment.

The process of determining the approximate value of damages relevant to a claim (i.e., total of medical bills, cost of repair & replacement of damaged property) and considering the financial provisions of the insurance policy to decide on a fair settlement amount.
Coverage for financial loss because of the cancellation or postponement of a specific event due to weather or other unexpected cause beyond the control of the insured.

The non-admitted market for coverage that the admitted market won't or can't write — high-risk accounts, unusual exposures, large limits, or new industries. More flexible pricing and terms but without state guaranty fund backing.
Provision in an insurance policy that indicates what is denied coverage. Examples of Exclusions are: Wear and Tear, Rust, Rot, Contamination, Mechanical Breakdown.
The percentage of premium used to run the insurance operation — commissions, underwriting expenses, taxes, and general operating costs. Formula: Underwriting Expenses ÷ Written Premium.

A multiplier that adjusts a business's workers compensation premium based on its actual loss experience versus the industry average for its classification. A Mod of 1.00 is average; below 1.00 means better-than-average (credit); above 1.00 means worse-than-average (debit).
A rating plan that adjusts premium based on the insured's own past loss experience. Most common in workers compensation (the Mod) and commercial auto fleets.
The date on which an insurance policy expires.

The right to solicit renewal of insurance accounts. In independent agency, the agency owns the expirations; in captive agency, the company typically owns them. Critical distinction affecting agency value and producer mobility.

Anything on or near a property that increases the risk of loss — a neighboring gas station, an adjacent vacant building, proximity to wildfire-prone areas, a chemical plant nearby. External exposures can make an otherwise good risk unacceptable.
An endorsement extending business income coverage past physical restoration of the property to include the time needed to rebuild the customer base — typically 30-365 days.
An additional form of protection that can be purchased to extend/enhance a manufacturer's warranty on consumer goods, like phones and electronics.
Pays extraordinary costs to continue operations after a property loss — renting temporary space, expedited shipping, overtime. Usually written alongside business income.
Property insurance for extraordinary expenses incurred to continue operations after a loss — for example, a backup generator rental during a power failure, or leasing temporary facilities.
The value printed on the face of a stock, bond, or other financial instrument or document.

A state-mandated insurance pool that writes property coverage for applicants who cannot obtain coverage in the voluntary market due to high risk (e.g., wildfire-prone areas, older urban buildings). "Fair Access to Insurance Requirements."

Systems to prevent or arrest falls from heights. OSHA requires fall protection when workers are 4' above lower levels (general industry) or 6' (construction). Options include guardrails, safety nets, and personal fall arrest systems.
The U.S. corporation insuring deposits in US banks against bank failure.
The process of cutting down standing trees. Highest-risk activity in logging. Controlled felling techniques, proper notch and backcut, planned escape routes, and buddy system are core controls.
Insurance purchased by an employer to protect against financial loss from dishonest acts by persons entrusted with valuable property or funds.
Coverage for claims against those who manage employee benefit plans for breaches of fiduciary duty under ERISA. Separate from D&O and crime coverage.
A state rate regulation allowing insurers to use new rates immediately upon filing them with the insurance department, subject to later review and potential disapproval.
A state statute requiring motorists to show financial capacity to pay for automobile-related losses — typically satisfied by carrying auto liability insurance at state-required minimums.
Detection, notification, and monitoring of fire events. Smoke detectors, heat detectors, pull stations, horn/strobes, and connection to a central station monitoring service.
Coverage for loss of or damage to a building and/or contents due to fire or lightning. Often includes business interruption and loss of rents.

Coverage for property loss liability resulting from negligent acts or omissions of the insured that allow a fire to spread and cause damage to others' property — common for tenants who may damage a landlord's building.
A pump that boosts water pressure to a sprinkler or standpipe system when municipal water pressure is insufficient. Must be tested annually. Failure can make sprinklers ineffective.
ISO Construction Class 6. Structural components have specific fire-resistance ratings (typically 2+ hours). Most favorable property rates.
The first person or entity named on the declarations page of a policy.
A claim that a policyholder files with her own insurer (as opposed to a third party claim, which the claimant files with someone else's insurer).
A situation involving only the insurer and insured.

term: First Step in Risk Management Decision Process
definition: Analyze loss exposures — identifying what the organization could lose, how it could happen, and what the financial consequences would be.
Cancellation of a policy effective back to its inception date, with a full premium refund. Treats the policy as if it never existed.

A structured program to reduce auto losses in a business with multiple vehicles. Includes driver selection criteria, MVR checks, driver training, vehicle maintenance, GPS/telematics monitoring, accident investigation, and disciplinary policies.

Generally excluded from standard property policies. Available through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) or private flood insurers. Has a waiting period (typically 30 days) unless purchased with a mortgage closing.

Coverage protecting the insured against loss or damage to real or personal property from flood. Flood is typically excluded from standard property policies and must be purchased separately, usually through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).
The federal agency within the Department of Transportation that regulates interstate commercial motor carriers. Issues safety regulations, investigates carriers, and tracks safety performance.
A federal database of drug and alcohol violations by commercial drivers. Employers must query the Clearinghouse before hiring and annually for current drivers.

A systematic method for identifying potential failures in a process or system and evaluating their effects. Each failure mode is scored for severity, occurrence, and detection; the product is a Risk Priority Number (RPN) used to prioritize corrective action.
Retention of a risk that occurs because insurance is unavailable, too expensive, or the exposure was not identified before the loss.
Establishing priorities by predicting frequency and severity of potential losses and estimating the costs of different risk management techniques.

State forestry regulations (Oregon OAR 437, Washington WAC 296-54, California 8 CCR 6250, Idaho IDAPA 17, Maine 12 MRSA) that govern logging safety, worker training, equipment standards, and environmental protection.
Using a specialized machine to grind vegetation in place rather than removing it. Different hazard profile than traditional logging — thrown debris, machine rollover on slopes.

The federal endorsement to a motor carrier's liability policy that guarantees payment of public liability (bodily injury, property damage, environmental) judgments up to statutory minimums — even if the underlying policy would otherwise exclude coverage. Required for for-hire interstate carriers.

term: Four Requirements of a Legal Contract
definition: Agreement, Consideration, Competent Parties, and Legal Purpose.
Collision, Comprehensive, Medical, and Liability.
Strategic, Operational, Reputational, and Compliance.
ISO Construction Class 1. Wood-framed buildings. Highest fire loss potential; highest rates.

Non-profit, mutual aid organizations that engage primarily in charitable or benevolent activities. They offer their members insurance against death, disease, and disability. Also known as Fraternal Associations.
Fraud is any intentional lying or misrepresentation by policyholders or claim adjusters in order to inflate a claims payment or receive a claims payment that would otherwise not be paid.
Deliberately faking or causing an incident in order to collect money.
Inflating the gravity or the extent of claims in order to receive a higher indemnification.
Flexible Spending Account. An account set up through an employer to cover health costs with pretax dollars that has very little or no rollover from year to year.

Designed to cover dealerships, auto repair shops, and garages for day-to-day operations. Includes auto, truck, motorcycle, RV, truck-tractor, and trailer dealers. Includes liability (casualty), garage keepers (property), and physical damage (property) coverage. UM, UIM, and medical payments may be added by endorsement.
Covers damage to customers' property that the insured has for servicing, repair, parking, or storage.
Covers both auto and business liability arising out of: (1) the ownership, maintenance, or use of covered autos; (2) garage operations (e.g., a customer falling at the insured's premises).
Comprehensive, specified causes of loss, and collision. Typically auto dealers purchase physical damage coverage on a blanket basis.

Covers bodily injury or property damage caused during business operations. Covers incidents on the business premises, slip-and-fall accidents, damage to customer property, and advertising injury. Sometimes called Commercial General Liability (CGL).

The accounting rules most companies use for general financial reporting. Insurers use GAAP for SEC filings and SAP for state regulators. GAAP is more focused on matching revenue to expenses in the period earned.
25/50/25 — $25,000 bodily injury per person, $50,000 bodily injury per accident, $25,000 property damage per accident.

A discount offered to drivers who have been licensed for a specified period (often 3+ years), have minimal violations on their record, and have not been found at fault in accidents causing bodily injury. Required in some states.
Acting honestly and fairly, with good intentions and full disclosure.

An insurance program where risks are transferred to a government agency. Also known as social insurance. It usually exists in situations where the exposure to loss is too high for private insurers to reliably insure risks.

A specified period immediately following the premium due date during which a payment can be made to continue a policy in force without interruption. Applies only to life and health policies. Check your policy for specifics.
The total cost of an insurance policy. Formula: Expected Losses + Risk Charge + Administrative Loading.
Ability to write off premiums as a true risk transfer, provided the parent's own risk constitutes no more than 30% of the total risk portfolio.

A state-operated fund that pays claims of insolvent insurers. Funded through assessments on licensed (admitted) insurers. The reason buying from an admitted insurer provides extra consumer protection.
Workers who fell trees with chainsaws rather than mechanized equipment. Highest exposure to struck-by hazards; required to complete state-certified training in many jurisdictions.
A period in the insurance cycle characterized by high demand and low supply — premiums rise, underwriting tightens, coverage becomes harder to place.
A condition that increases the likelihood or severity of a loss from a peril. Physical hazards (icy walkway), moral hazards (dishonesty), and morale hazards (carelessness).

OSHA's "right to know" standard requiring employers to inform workers about chemical hazards in the workplace through labels, Safety Data Sheets, and training. Also called the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) after the 2012 update.
Any legal action that increases the chance of a loss.
Conscious and deliberate action on the part of the insured that increases the chance of a loss. All conscious acts committed with a hope to defraud the insurance company are moral hazards.
Unconscious tendency of insured people to engage in riskier behavior.
Any physical condition that increases the chance of loss.

The preferred order for controlling workplace hazards, from most to least effective: (1) Elimination — remove the hazard entirely; (2) Substitution — replace with something safer; (3) Engineering controls — isolate people from the hazard; (4) Administrative controls — change how people work; (5) PPE — personal protective equipment as the last line of defense.
A categorization of workplace hazards commonly used in risk assessment: physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychosocial. Each requires different controls.
Health Maintenance Organization. A health insurance plan in which patients receive health care from designated providers.
A type of Homeowner's policy that provides coverage to both Coverage A (Structure) and Coverage B (Personal Property) on a Named Peril basis.

A type of homeowner's policy that provides coverage to Coverage A (Structure) for All Risks of Physical Loss, unless specifically excluded, and to Coverage B (Personal Property) on a Named Peril basis.

A contract provision where one party agrees to assume the liability of another for specified losses. Can be limited (only for the indemnitor's fault), intermediate (indemnitor's and indemnitee's joint fault), or broad (all liability, even the indemnitee's sole fault). Enforceability varies by state.
A form of property insurance, available to property owners, that covers losses to the physical structure as well as to their personal property and protects the insured person from liability claims.
A document issued before cutting or welding, listing required precautions: removal of combustibles, fire extinguisher present, fire watch during and 30+ minutes after work. Signed by a supervisor.

A written program controlling cutting, welding, brazing, grinding, and other activities that generate heat or sparks. Includes hot work permits, fire watch, and area preparation. Required to prevent fires — a leading cause of industrial losses.

FMCSA regulations limiting the hours commercial drivers can operate to reduce fatigue-related crashes. Includes the 11-hour driving limit, 14-hour on-duty window, 30-minute break requirement, and 60/70-hour weekly limits.
The practice of maintaining an orderly, clean workplace. Critical for fire safety (removing combustibles), slip-and-fall prevention, and efficient operations. A common loss-control recommendation.
Health Savings Account. An account where you contribute pre-tax dollars for expected medical expenses.
Coverage for damage to a vessel (ship, boat, or aircraft) and affixed items. The marine equivalent of property damage coverage for the vehicle itself.
A felled tree that has not fallen fully to the ground because it was caught on another tree. Extremely dangerous; requires specialized release techniques. Never climbed or worked under.
A compact among U.S. states and Canadian provinces that simplifies fuel tax reporting for motor carriers operating across jurisdictions. Carriers report once and taxes are allocated to states.
The possible effect on the organization if a risk event occurs. Measured in dollars, lost operational capacity, reputation damage, or other terms depending on the risk.
Involves both technical decisions (which controls to install, what coverage to buy) and managerial decisions (budget, staffing, communication).
The structured process of examining an accident, injury, or near miss to determine what happened, why it happened, and what should be done to prevent recurrence.

A life insurance and annuity provision limiting the time within which the insurer has the legal right to void the contract on grounds of material misrepresentation in the application — usually two years.

Claims that have occurred but the insurer has not yet been notified of them at the reporting date. Insurers maintain reserves for IBNR to fund future claim payments. Critical for accurate financial reporting.
Expenses that have already been sustained and that have not yet been paid. Additional Living Expenses payments are a reimbursement of Incurred Expenses.
Reimbursement for a loss, which leaves the claimant in the same financial position that she was in before the loss.
A contract provision requiring one party to protect another from specified losses, claims, or damages. Often paired with an insurance requirement to fund the indemnity obligation.
To restore by payment, repair, or replacement.
Insurance that indemnifies loss as opposed to liability.

A general legal principle that an individual recovering under an insurance policy should be restored to approximately the same financial position they were in prior to the loss — not profit from the loss.
A freelance contractor paid a fee for adjusting losses on behalf of insurance companies. Not an employee of any single insurer.
A representative of multiple insurance companies who sells and services policies. Owns the book of business and operates under the American Agency System.
Extends personal auto coverage to immediate family members of the named insured.

The science of recognizing, evaluating, and controlling occupational health hazards — noise, chemicals, biological agents, ergonomic stressors. Industrial hygienists conduct air sampling, noise monitoring, and ergonomic assessments.
Pays lost business income when physical access to the insured's premises is blocked by a covered cause of loss, even when the premises itself is not damaged.

Coverage for property that may be in transit, held by a bailee, at a fixed location, a movable good often at different locations (e.g., off-road construction equipment), or scheduled property (antiques, fine art). Also covers instrumentalities of transportation and communication such as bridges, tunnels, piers, pipelines, and radio/TV towers.
Coverage for materials and equipment while in transit and until installed at a job site. Used by contractors installing machinery, HVAC, or building components.

A right or relationship with the subject matter of the insurance such that the insured can suffer a financial loss from damage to, or destruction of, it. Required for an insurance contract to be legally valid — you cannot insure something you do not have a stake in.
An agreement where you make regular payments to a company and the company promises to pay you money if you suffer a specified injury, illness, loss, etc.
An employee of an insurance company who sells insurance policies and provides customer service.
The head of a state's department of insurance. Elected in some states, appointed in others. Responsible for enforcing the state's insurance code, licensing, and consumer protection.
Company which sells insurance policies to individuals or to other companies.
An act on the part of either the buyer or seller of an insurance contract in an attempt to gain money illegally.
A contract in which an individual or entity receives financial protection or reimbursement against losses.

term: Insurance — Uncertainties as a Risk Financing Method
definition: Inadequate policy limits, disagreements with insurers over coverage, delays in claim payment, and insurer insolvency.
The party to an insurance arrangement whom the insurer agrees to indemnify for losses, provide benefits for, or render services.
The insurance company.
An insurer licensed by the state to sell specific lines of insurance within the state (cannot sell surplus lines insurance). Also referred to as an Authorized Insurer.
An insurer that adheres to the laws of another country besides the U.S. and its territories.
An insurer that adheres to the laws of a particular state and is housed in that state.
An insurer that has permission to issue policies in a state, but is not housed in that state.
Nonadmitted (or unauthorized) insurers that do not have permission to sell surplus lines insurance.
An insurer not licensed in the state (can sometimes sell surplus lines insurance). Also referred to as an Unauthorized Insurer.
The main part of a car insurance policy, which clearly spells out every point of what coverage you receive for the premium you're paying.
Property that consists of legal rights rather than physical objects, such as securities, trademarks, copyrights, patents, and licenses.
Direct financial interest in protecting a unit.

A separate occurrence that "intervenes" between a defendant's actions and damage or loss to another person. An intervening cause may decrease the defendant's liability, if the defendant can show that it was the intervening cause (rather than the defendant himself) that caused the damage or loss.
A reciprocal agreement that allows apportioned registration of commercial vehicles based on miles operated in each jurisdiction. One plate and cab card covers all participating jurisdictions.
A life insurance beneficiary who has a vested interest in the policy proceeds even during the insured's lifetime. The policyholder may only change the beneficiary with the beneficiary's consent.

An advisory organization that develops standard insurance policy forms (such as the Commercial General Liability form), provides rating information, and compiles industry loss statistics. Most U.S. commercial insurers use ISO forms or close variations.

International standard for risk management. Proactive and applicable to a broad range of organizations; maximizes value across a range of organizational stakeholders. Defines risk as the effect of uncertainty on objectives (positive OR negative).
"The effect of uncertainty on objectives." This definition embraces both positive (upside) and negative (downside) outcomes.
"Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to risk."

term: Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) / Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
definition: A technique that breaks a job down into its basic steps, identifies hazards at each step, and develops controls. Foundational tool in industrial safety and required for many OSHA-regulated operations.
A metric that signals when risk exposure may be increasing. KRIs are forward-looking — they help management spot emerging risks before they cause losses.

A life (and sometimes disability) policy purchased by a business on the life of a person integral to its operations — a founder, top executive, or star salesperson. The business is both the owner and beneficiary.
Coverage for ransom or extortion costs and related expenses when an insured person or employee is kidnapped or the business is extorted.
A risk that is expected and involves little uncertainty — for example, employee injuries at a factory. Managed with routine controls and workers' compensation.
A risk that is known to exist but whose probability or impact is uncertain — for example, knowing that a hurricane could strike but not knowing when or how severe.

Backward-looking metrics measuring actual loss performance — injury rates, claim counts, lost workdays, OSHA recordable incidents. Useful for measuring results but cannot prevent the losses they measure.
The area where logs are gathered, sorted, and loaded onto trucks. Hazards include struck-by (log movement, equipment), caught-between, and traffic exposure.
Termination of a policy due to failure to pay the required renewal premium.
A principle of insurance: it is more efficient to insure against large, infrequent losses than small, predictable ones. Administrative cost of small claims often exceeds the benefit.

One of the requirements of an insurable risk — insurers need a large pool of similar exposures to accurately predict losses. Example: a life insurer selling only to one age bracket and health class to get reliable statistics.

Forward-looking metrics that predict future safety performance — percentage of employees trained, number of safety inspections completed, near-miss reports filed. Allow intervention before losses occur.
The insured cannot take legal action against the insurance company unless the insured has fully complied with all terms of the policy.
One of the four qualifications of a legally binding contract. The purpose of the contract must be legal (e.g., no contracts for illicit drug deals).
Responsibility to another for one's negligence.
Auto insurance that protects you against medical costs for the bodily injury of others and damages to the property of others if you are at fault in an accident.
Liability insurance indemnifies a third party for damages caused by the insured's negligence.
Legal actions against the insured, including investigation costs, defense costs, and damages (general and special).
Insurance paid to named beneficiaries when the insured person dies.
The limits of an insurance policy represent the highest amount an insurer will pay. Any cost above the limits of insurance is the responsibility of the policyholder.

The total amount the insurer will pay for all losses during the policy period. Often appears preceded by an occurrence limit (e.g., 100,000/1,000,000). The first number indicates the maximum payout for covered losses in any single occurrence; the second number indicates the total amount the insurer will pay for all covered occurrences during the policy term.
Establishes one all-inclusive limit, or maximum payout, for liability damages caused by the policyholder. Contrast with split limits.

An insurance provision that specifies different coverage limits for different parts of a claim. The most common example is split limits in a personal auto policy, which includes three different limits of liability: (1) maximum limit for bodily injury for any one individual, (2) maximum limit for bodily injury to multiple people in any one accident or occurrence, and (3) maximum limit for property damage in any one accident or occurrence.

Coverage for an entity involved in the retail or wholesale sale of alcoholic beverages, or the serving of alcoholic beverages, to persons who have incurred bodily injury or property damage arising from an intoxicated person. Also called "dram shop" coverage.

The amount that can be borrowed at a specified interest rate from the issuing company by a life insurance policyholder, using the policy's cash value as collateral. If the policyholder dies with the loan outstanding, the amount borrowed plus interest is deducted from the death benefit.
OSHA-required procedure for controlling hazardous energy during equipment maintenance. Workers apply physical locks and warning tags to prevent unexpected startup that could cause injury or death.
A mobile crane or grapple used to load logs onto trucks at the landing. Requires operator certification and daily pre-use inspection.

The high-hazard industry involving felling, bucking, yarding, loading, and hauling timber. Subject to specialized OSHA standard 29 CFR 1910.266 and state-specific logging codes (OR-OSHA, WA L&I, CA Cal/OSHA).

Narrow forest roads, steep grades, off-highway tire-to-highway tire transitions, load securement, bridge weight limits, and wildlife strikes. Often requires specialty driver training and specific endorsements.

Coverage for services provided in a setting other than an acute care unit of a hospital — nursing home care, home health care, assisted living, or community-based care. Often includes benefits for cognitive impairment or loss of functional capacity.

Policy providing monthly income payments for insureds who become disabled for an extensive length of time, typically two years or longer. Benefits continue until recovery, retirement, or a specified age.
When someone with insurance endures a negative outcome, such as injury or theft, and expects payment from the insurance company to compensate them.
Costs incurred to investigate, settle, and pay insurance claims. Includes defense costs, adjuster fees, expert witness fees, and internal claims-handling expenses.

The practice of identifying, evaluating, and reducing the frequency and/or severity of losses. Performed by insurance company loss control consultants, brokers, and internal risk managers. Includes surveys, recommendations, training, and follow-up audits.

A written report documenting a loss control survey — the risk's operations, existing controls, hazards identified, recommendations, and the consultant's overall risk evaluation. Often used by underwriting to price and accept the account.
An on-site visit by a loss control consultant to observe operations, identify hazards, evaluate controls, and make written recommendations. Results in a loss control report shared with underwriting.

The change over time in estimates of what an insurer will ultimately pay for claims from a given accident period. Newer claims are often underestimated; as time passes, more accurate figures emerge. Actuaries use "loss triangles" to track development.
The chance of financial loss due to some peril. Every loss exposure has three elements: the exposed value (what could be lost), the peril (what could cause the loss), and the financial consequence.

Estimating the frequency and severity of future losses using historical data, trend analysis, industry benchmarks, and stochastic modeling. Essential for retention decisions, budget planning, and insurance buying strategy.

Usually an inverse relationship — as frequency increases, severity typically decreases, and vice versa. High-frequency/low-severity losses are candidates for retention; low-frequency/high-severity losses are candidates for transfer via insurance.

Refers to an insured's history of losses (claims) with other companies, or the company they are currently with. A company will consider "loss history" when underwriting a new policy or considering a renewal of an existing policy. Companies view "loss history" as an indication of an insured's propensity for a claim in the future.

A formal report from a carrier showing all claims an insured has had over a period — typically 3-5 years. Includes dates, descriptions, amounts paid, and reserves. Standard document requested during underwriting and at renewal.
The party to whom money or insurance proceeds is to be paid in the event of loss, such as the lien holder or an automobile or the mortgagee on real property.
Measures taken BEFORE a loss to reduce the frequency of losses (e.g., fire safety training for kitchen staff before their first shift).
The percentage of premium an insurer pays out in losses and loss adjustment expenses. Formula: Incurred Losses ÷ Earned Premium. A key measure of how well an insurer is underwriting.
Measures taken to reduce the severity of losses. Pre-loss reduction (e.g., sprinkler systems installed in advance) or post-loss reduction (e.g., quick emergency response).
An amount the insurer sets aside on its books to cover claims that have been reported but not yet paid, plus estimates for IBNR. Critical to measuring financial solvency.
A table showing how claims from each accident year (or reporting period) develop over successive valuation dates. Used by actuaries to project ultimate losses and set reserves.
Physical harm to tangible property caused by a peril.
An economic loss that results from the direct, or physical, loss.

Coverage protecting a licensed health care provider or professional against legal liability resulting from death, injury, or loss to a patient or client caused by the insured's negligence, error, or omission in rendering professional services. Also called Medical Professional Liability.

An umbrella term for coverages protecting managers and the entity from management-related claims: D&O (directors and officers), EPLI (employment practices), Fiduciary Liability (employee benefit plan administration), and Crime.

A provision in a blanket property policy limiting recovery at any one location to a percentage (typically 110-125%) of the reported value at that location — preventing all the blanket limit from going to a single loss.

Coverage for goods in transit and the vehicles of transportation on waterways, land, and air. Split into Ocean Marine (ships, cargo across oceans) and Inland Marine (goods moving over land and scheduled personal property).
The price something will sell for in the open market, or "fair market value."
A significant misstatement in an application form. If a company had access to the correct information at the time of application, the company might not have agreed to accept the application.
An authority number issued by FMCSA for for-hire motor carriers transporting federally regulated commodities interstate. Separate from the DOT number.
The form motor carriers file biennially to update their operational information with FMCSA. Required to maintain active operating authority.
Provides public liability coverage for bodily injury, property damage, and environmental protection. Federally required endorsement for motor carriers.

Coverage for repair or replacement of property that fails due to defects in materials, workmanship, or normal wear and tear. Commonly applied to motor vehicles, mobile equipment, appliances, and electronics.

Logging using feller-bunchers, harvesters, forwarders, and processors — equipment that replaces much of the hand-cutting with enclosed-cab machine operation. Generally safer than hand-cutting but introduces equipment-specific hazards.
A federal and state assistance program that pays for health care services for people who cannot afford them.

Auto coverage that pays reasonable expenses incurred for necessary medical and funeral services because of bodily injury caused by an accident and sustained by you or any other person while occupying a covered automobile — regardless of fault.

A federal assistance program, passed under Title XVIII of the Social Security Amendments of 1965, that provides hospital and medical expense insurance to people over 65 and certain disabled individuals.

Insurance coverage sold on an individual or group basis to help fill the "gaps" in Medicare coverage. Pays part or all of Medicare's deductibles and co-payments and may cover services not covered by Medicare. Cannot duplicate Medicare benefits.
Standardized questionnaires, review of financial statements, flowcharts of operations, and personal inspections of the premises.

An agent or agency with greater authority from an insurance company than a typical agent — may include underwriting, policy issuance, claims handling, and producer appointment authority. Effectively operates as an outsourced underwriting arm.
The lowest amount of auto insurance coverage one can have to be legally allowed to drive in each state.
Any false or misleading statement.
Planning of specific actions to reduce the likelihood or impact of identified risks. Occurs after analysis of probability, impact, and speed of onset.
When this endorsement is attached, mobile equipment is considered a covered auto.
Temporary alternative job assignments within the injured worker's medical restrictions while they recover. Enables return-to-work before full release to normal duties.
After implementation, results must be monitored and adjusted as exposures change, new risks emerge, or techniques prove ineffective.
Moody's Investors Service's rating of an insurer's claims-paying ability. Ranges from Aaa (highest quality) to C. One of several rating agencies commercial and reinsurance buyers consult.

Personality characteristics or intentional behavior that increase the probability of loss — for example, an insured filing a fraudulent claim, or deliberately destroying property to collect insurance proceeds. Conscious and deliberate.
Negligence or carelessness on the part of the insured that increases the probability of loss — for example, not locking doors because "I have insurance." Unconscious or careless, not deliberate.
Insurance that indemnifies a lender for loss upon foreclosure if a borrower fails to meet required mortgage payments. Also called Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI).
A form of life insurance payable to a third-party lender upon the death of the insured mortgagor. Pays off the remaining mortgage balance.
Requires trucking companies to certify that they are able to meet financial obligations if they become liable for injury or damage arising from their trucking operations.

term: Motor Carrier Act — Limits of Liability
definition: Affects vehicles with a gross weight of 10,000 lbs or more. Required limits are divided into three categories: (1) $750,000 for interstate transportation of non-hazardous property; (2) $5,000,000 for interstate/intrastate transportation of large quantities of certain hazardous materials (compressed gas, radioactive materials, explosives); (3) $1,000,000 for interstate/intrastate transportation of oil or other hazardous materials.
For businesses that transport their own goods and property and may haul the goods of others. Owned passenger autos may be covered autos in the motor carrier coverage form.
Coverage sold to municipalities to guarantee the principal and interest payments on bonds issued — enhances the bond's credit rating.
An insurance company that is owned by its policyholders who participate in dividends. These companies have no shareholders and are not traded publicly.
A parent company organized as a mutual, owning one or more stock insurers. Allows the benefits of both mutual ownership (by policyholders) and stock access to capital markets.
A state-issued driving record showing violations, accidents, license status, and endorsements. Reviewed as part of driver hiring and ongoing fleet management.

The U.S. standard-setting body created by the chief insurance regulators of all 50 states, D.C., and 5 U.S. territories. Sets model laws, financial reporting standards, and conducts peer review. Insurance is state-regulated, but the NAIC coordinates standards across states.
The individual, persons, or entities defined as the insured on the declarations page of the policy. The "first named insured" generally has additional rights and responsibilities.
A policy that names all perils that will be covered. Contrasts with open-peril policies.

The rating and statistical organization for workers compensation insurance in most U.S. states. Develops class codes, loss costs, and the experience rating formula that produces a business's Experience Mod (EMR).
An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage but had the potential to do so. Reporting and investigating near misses is a key leading indicator.
The failure to use that degree of care which an ordinary person of reasonable prudence would use under similar circumstances.

The defendant had a legal duty to act (or not act) in a prescribed manner; the defendant failed to act accordingly (called breach of duty); the plaintiff suffered actual loss or injury due to the defendant's action or inaction; and the loss or injury to the plaintiff was a direct result of the breach of duty of the defendant.
Reduction in net income caused by an event that either decreases revenues, increases expenses, or both. Often covered by business income insurance.

An insurance company that is NOT licensed in the state where the risk is located. Non-admitted carriers can write "surplus lines" business — coverage unavailable from admitted markets. Not backed by state guaranty funds. Also called "Unauthorized" or "Surplus Lines" carrier.
ISO Construction Class 3. Walls and structural components are non-combustible but may not have extensive fire-resistance ratings (e.g., bare steel-framed building).
Not-for-profit insurance companies that return profits to policyholders by reducing premiums or expanding benefits.
An insurer's decision not to renew a policy at expiration. Most states require advance written notice (typically 30-60 days). Different from cancellation, which ends a policy mid-term.
An agreement that the insurer asks the claimant to sign which states that the insurer's investigation of the loss can't be used later on as proof that the loss is covered under the policy.

One of the requirements of an insurable risk — the peril cannot cause simultaneous losses to a large portion of the insured pool. This is why private insurers decline to offer standalone flood coverage.
Notice required by insurance companies immediately after an accident or other loss. Part of the standard provisions defining a policyholder's responsibilities after a loss.
The party protected by a surety bond. The person, firm, or corporation for whose benefit the bond is issued.
The party whose performance is guaranteed by a surety bond. Under a bond, strictly speaking, both the principal and the surety are obligors.
A classification of a building based on what's happening inside — office, retail, warehouse, manufacturing, restaurant. Drives both property rates (fire hazard) and liability rates (public exposure).
An event that results in an insured loss or damages.
A liability insurance form that covers claims arising from an incident that occurred during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is reported. Contrasts with a "claims-made" form.

Coverage for goods in transit by ocean vessel, typically written on a "warehouse-to-warehouse" basis. Covers perils of the sea, fire, jettison, and usually extended to include more perils under institute cargo clauses.
Coverage for ocean and inland water transportation exposures: cargo, ships and hulls, earnings, and liability. One of the oldest forms of insurance.

A single insurance program purchased by a project owner that covers all contractors and subcontractors on a construction project. Provides consistent coverage and avoids gaps between multiple contractor policies.
An open perils policy covers all causes of loss except for those explicitly excluded. Also known as "All Peril."
A deliberate decision to retain a risk, usually because the expected loss is small, predictable, or cheaper to absorb than to insure.

The insurer has three options for settling physical damage claims: (1) pay for, repair, or replace the damaged or stolen property; (2) return stolen property to the insured at the company's expense and pay for any damages resulting from the theft; (3) take the damaged or stolen property at an agreed or appraised value.

The record of workplace injuries and illnesses that most employers with more than 10 employees must maintain. Includes a 300 log (detailed record), 300A annual summary (posted Feb 1 – Apr 30), and 301 incident reports.

An injury or illness that must be recorded on the OSHA 300 log. Generally includes cases involving medical treatment beyond first aid, lost time, restricted work, loss of consciousness, or significant diagnosed conditions.
States that the business auto coverage form provides primary coverage for covered autos owned by the insured; the coverage is excess for losses involving non-owned covered autos.
The maximum amount of money you will have to spend on health care in a year, after which the insurance company agrees to cover all costs.
Modify the policy's liability coverage to meet other states' financial responsibility requirements (minimum limits of liability) when the covered auto is being driven in that state.
A declaration that a vehicle or driver cannot operate until violations are corrected. Issued during roadside inspections or compliance reviews for serious safety defects.
The amount of money dedicated to a general contractor's cost of doing business and earning a profit. Insurance industry standard is 10% and 10%.

Two or more distinct coverages combined into a single contract — for example, a Homeowners policy combines property, personal property, and liability coverages. Typically cheaper than buying each coverage separately.
