The direct cause of loss — fire, windstorm, theft, collision. Sometimes confused with "hazard."
These are the specific causes of loss that are covered under the policy.

Coverage designed to insure private passenger automobiles and certain types of trucks owned by an individual or married couple. Includes liability, medical payments, uninsured/underinsured motorist, comprehensive, and collision coverages.

Credit insurance that pays the difference between the outstanding balance owed on a vehicle loan and the actual cash value of the vehicle in the event of a total loss. Prevents the borrower from owing money on a car they no longer own.

Liability coverage for claims arising from discrimination, false arrest, illegal detention, libel, slander, malicious prosecution, mental anguish, identity theft, alienation of affections, or violation of privacy. Broader than "bodily injury."

Auto insurance that protects you against the cost of your own medical bills and, in some states, lost wages and replacement services if you are involved in a car accident, regardless of fault. Required in no-fault states.
First-hand site visits to assess risk exposure, observe operations, and identify hazards that may not appear in documents.

Equipment worn by workers to minimize exposure to hazards — hard hats, safety glasses, hearing protection, gloves, respirators, high-visibility vests, fall protection harnesses. Required by OSHA when engineering and administrative controls are insufficient.
The financial exposure created when key personnel die, quit, retire, or become disabled. Addressed through succession planning and key person insurance.
A veterinary care insurance policy providing coverage for a pet animal (dog, cat, etc.) in the event of illness or accident. May cover routine care, accidents, or both.
Contains three principal coverages: (1) Collision, (2) Comprehensive, (3) Specified Causes of Loss.

Excluded: sound reproducing and receiving equipment, tapes, and records; wear and tear, freezing, mechanical or electrical breakdown, or road damage to tires; covered autos while being used for organized or professional racing, demolition, and stunting activities.
Contract between an insurance company and policyholder that explains the amounts and types of coverage.

term: Policy Administration System / Agency Management System (AMS)
definition: The software system an agency uses to manage policies, customer records, billing, claims, and workflow. Major systems include Applied Epic, Applied TAM, Vertafore AMS360, and Vertafore Sagitta.
The period a policy is in force, from the beginning or effective date to the expiration date.
The individual or firm buying the policy.

Any solid, liquid, gaseous, or thermal irritant or contaminant including smoke, vapor, soot, fumes, acids, alkalis, chemicals, and waste. Waste includes material to be recycled, reconditioned, or reclaimed.

Coverage for third-party bodily injury, property damage, and cleanup costs from pollution incidents. Most general liability policies exclude pollution; a separate policy is required for operations with environmental exposure.
Authority given to one person or corporation to act for and obligate another, to the extent laid down in the instrument creating the power.

Preferred Provider Organization. A health insurance plan in which providers agree to deliver services for discount fees; patients can go to any provider, but using nonparticipating providers results in higher costs to the patient.

A DOT-required walk-around inspection of a commercial vehicle before each shift — brakes, lights, tires, fluid levels, coupling, load security. Documented on a Driver Vehicle Inspection Report (DVIR).

Coverage for liability of an insured to persons who have incurred bodily injury or property damage on an insured's premises during normal operations, routine maintenance, or from the insured's business operations either on or off the premises. Core coverage in a CGL policy.
The amount paid by an insured to an insurance company to obtain or maintain an insurance policy.

A policy provision (and certificate requirement) that the insurance will respond before and without seeking contribution from the certificate holder's own insurance. Common contract requirement when one party is an additional insured.
Minimizing loss — reducing the financial impact of uncertainty on an organization through identification, assessment, and treatment of risk exposures.
Coverage that takes precedence when more than one policy covers the same loss. Primary pays first; excess or umbrella policies kick in after primary limits are exhausted.

A state rate regulation requiring insurers to obtain explicit approval from the insurance department before using a new rate or form. Contrasts with "file-and-use" states where insurers can use rates upon filing.
The chance of something happening or not happening within a certain number of occurrences.
A general term for any licensed individual who sells, services, or negotiates insurance — includes agents, brokers, and solicitors. Some states use "producer" as a single unified license.
The contract between an agent and an insurance company defining terms of the relationship — authorities granted, commission schedule, termination rights, and ownership of expirations.
The license issued by a state that permits an individual or agency to sell, solicit, or negotiate insurance. Requires pre-licensing education, passing an exam, and continuing education to maintain.

Coverage protecting a manufacturer, distributor, seller, or lessor of a product against legal liability resulting from a defective condition causing personal injury or damage to any individual or entity.
Coverage for the costs of recalling a defective product — transportation, storage, disposal, and customer communications. Often excluded from general liability; purchased separately by manufacturers.

Protects professionals (designers, architects, engineers, consultants, insurance agents) against claims of negligence, errors, or omissions in their professional services. Essential for anyone providing advice or specialized services for a fee.
Coverage for architects, engineers, and surveyors against claims of professional negligence in their designs, plans, and specifications.
Damage to another person's property. The purpose of liability insurance is to cover property damage to a third party resulting from the negligent or unintentional acts of the insured.
A broad form of marine legal liability insurance coverage. Covers third-party injuries and damages arising from vessel operations.

A 1-10 classification assigned to a community's fire protection capability. Class 1 is best (excellent fire department, hydrants, water supply); Class 10 means little or no recognized fire protection. Directly impacts property insurance rates.
The clauses that make up the contract language of an insurance policy — define rights, duties, conditions, and limitations of both insurer and insured.

The direct, dominant cause that sets into motion a chain of events resulting in a loss, without the intervention of any other independent or unrelated source. Determines whether a loss is covered under a policy.
A person hired by the insured to settle the claim with the insurance company on their behalf.
The portion of a premium equal to expected losses, without any loading for insurance company expenses, premium taxes, contingencies, or profit margin.
A situation including possibility of loss or no loss, but no possibility of gain. Only pure risks are insurable — speculative risks (stock market gains/losses) are not.
An estimate of the cost of insurance based on information supplied by the applicant. A quote is not a binding contract — it is subject to underwriting approval.
A price estimate for coverage. A firm quote is subject to underwriting and terms; an "indication" is preliminary pricing given before full underwriting has been done.
The price per unit of insurance exposure. Premium = Rate × Exposure units. For example, $0.50 per square foot × 2,500 sq. ft. = $1,250 annual premium.

A formal submission to a state insurance department seeking approval (or providing notice) of proposed rates, rules, or forms. States use one of several approval methods: prior approval, file-and-use, use-and-file, or flex rating.

The process of calculating how much premium to charge for a type of insurance coverage. Considers expected losses, expenses, profit margin, and investment returns. Regulated by state insurance departments.
An organization that collects loss data from member insurers and develops loss costs and advisory rates used in pricing. ISO (property-casualty) and NCCI (workers compensation) are the largest.
A refund or offering of any part of the agent's commission to the insured as an inducement to buy. Illegal in most states because it discriminates between policyholders.
The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time — e.g., "we can afford to lose no more than 4 hours of data." Drives the frequency of backups and replication.
The target time within which a critical system or process must be restored after a disruption. Drives the design of recovery strategies and tools.
A submission that exceeds an underwriter's individual authority and must be referred to a senior underwriter or home office. Common for larger accounts or unusual risks.
Restoring a lapsed policy to full force and effect. Some insurers require evidence of insurability and payment of past-due premiums plus interest.

A transaction between a primary insurer and another licensed insurer (the reinsurer) where the reinsurer agrees to cover all or part of the losses of the primary insurer in exchange for a premium. Allows insurers to spread their own risk.
The insurance company that assumes reinsurance risk from another insurer (the ceding company).
Commission paid on policy renewals, typically at a lower rate than the new-business (first-year) commission in life insurance; generally the same rate in property-casualty.

A form of property insurance available to renters that covers losses to their personal property and protects them from liability claims. Does not cover the physical structure — that is the landlord's responsibility.
The cost associated with replacing property at current market prices.

Property coverage that pays the cost to repair or replace damaged property with new property of like kind and quality, without deduction for depreciation — subject to policy limits. Generally preferred over ACV for buildings and contents.
A statement by an applicant about a fact material to the insurance contract. Unlike a warranty, misrepresentations generally only void coverage if material and relied upon by the insurer.
A company's best estimate of what it will pay for claims. The amount representing actual or potential liabilities kept by an insurer to cover debts to policyholders.

The amount of uncertainty that remains after all possible risk management strategies have been applied. You cannot eliminate all risk; the residual is what the organization must live with (or transfer via insurance).
The agent or broker who deals directly with the insurance buyer (the insured). Contrasts with wholesale broker, who deals between retail and the carrier.
The portion of risk an organization keeps for itself rather than transferring to an insurer. Includes self-insurance, deductibles, and self-insured retentions (SIRs).
The maximum amount of any one claim, or total claims, that an insurer will carry on its own without turning to reinsurance.

A rating plan that determines final premium based on actual losses during the policy period, within a minimum and maximum range. Rewards insureds for low losses, penalizes for high losses. Common for large commercial accounts.
A formal program that brings injured workers back to modified duty as soon as medically appropriate. Reduces workers compensation costs, improves worker recovery, and maintains productivity.
A situation involving exposure to danger, harm, or loss.
The amount and type of risk an organization is willing to take in pursuit of its objectives. Expressed quantitatively (maximum loss tolerance) or qualitatively (risk categories to avoid entirely).

A grid plotting the probability of loss against the severity of loss. Used to prioritize risks — high-probability / high-severity risks get top attention; low-probability / low-severity may be retained or ignored.

The maximum amount of risk an organization can bear — tied to capital, liquidity, and operational resilience. Differs from risk appetite, which is the amount the organization *chooses* to take within its capacity.
Any measure that modifies risk by decreasing the likelihood that a risk event will occur, or reducing the impact the event would have on the organization.
Level of Risk = Probability of Occurrence × Magnitude of Impact. The fundamental formula used in risk assessment.
The category of risk management techniques that deal with paying for losses after they occur. Includes retention (self-funding) and contractual transfer (to an insurer or another party).
The spreading of financial risk evenly among a large number of contributors to the program.
A specific written suggestion from a loss control consultant to the insured to reduce exposure to loss. May be mandatory (required for continued coverage) or advisory. Tracked for completion.
A central document listing identified risks, their owners, probability, impact, existing controls, and treatment plans. A core tool of enterprise risk management.
The amount of risk the organization can survive if an event occurs. Tolerance defines the boundary past which a risk becomes unacceptable.

A regulatory formula that calculates the minimum amount of capital an insurance company should hold based on the risks it has taken (underwriting, investment, credit, and operational risks). Insurers falling below specified RBC thresholds face regulatory action. A key solvency measure.

An association organized for the purpose of absorbing losses through a risk-sharing mechanism, thereby limiting any single member's exposure. Common in high-risk markets (e.g., FAIR Plans for property insurance in high-risk regions).

A problem-solving method used to identify the underlying cause(s) of an incident — not just the immediate cause but the system or process failure that permitted it. Common tools include the "5 Whys" and fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams.
FMCSA's public database where shippers, brokers, and insurers can look up carrier safety records, insurance filings, and operational authority.

An FMCSA review of a new entrant carrier's safety operations within the first 12 months. Checks compliance with regulations on driver qualification, HOS, vehicle maintenance, and drug/alcohol testing.

A group of management and employee representatives that meets regularly to review safety performance, investigate incidents, and promote safe work practices. Required by law in some states for certain employer sizes.

The shared values, attitudes, and behaviors that determine an organization's commitment to safety. A strong safety culture is a leading indicator of reduced losses. Measured through perception surveys and behavioral observations.

A standardized document that communicates hazards of chemicals in the workplace. Required by OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard. Replaces the older "MSDS" (Material Safety Data Sheet). Must be accessible to all employees exposed to hazardous chemicals.
The value recoverable after a loss. For example, after an insurer pays a claim on a totaled vehicle, the insurer may take the wreck and sell it for salvage value.

A list attached to a policy identifying specific property or persons covered. For example, an inland marine schedule lists each piece of covered equipment; a scheduled personal property endorsement lists each jewelry item.

A rating plan that allows the underwriter to adjust the premium up or down based on specific characteristics of the risk that aren't reflected in the base rate — management controls, loss control programs, housekeeping, or adverse features.

term: Selection Criteria for Risk Management Techniques
definition: Effectiveness (how well the technique reduces risk) and economy (cost of the technique vs. expected loss).
A formal program where an organization retains its own risk and pays losses from its own funds. Works best when losses are predictable based on historical data.
Size of a loss; the extent of damages.
Coverage providing weekly or monthly income benefits for up to five years (individual) or up to one year (group) for full or partial disability arising from accident or sickness.
Ground-based yarding using a tractor or skidder to drag logs along the ground. High-risk operation requiring trained operators and proper equipment.
The residual woody debris left after logging — tops, branches, broken pieces. Fire hazard; often must be piled and burned or treated per state requirements.

The FMCSA database that compiles CSA data for carriers, assigning percentile scores in each BASIC category. Publicly viewable; affects insurance underwriting, shipper qualification, and enforcement priority.
A federal program that provides monthly benefits to millions of Americans, including retirees, military families, surviving families of deceased workers, and disabled individuals.
A period in the insurance cycle characterized by abundant supply of insurance capital driving premiums down. Underwriting becomes more flexible; coverage is easier to obtain.
An insurer's ability to pay all claims and debts as they come due. State regulators monitor solvency through financial statement filings and capital/surplus requirements.
A limit applied individually to each location or item. Subject to the coinsurance penalty if values are under-reported at a specific location.
Policy that provides benefits only for the diagnosis and/or treatment of a specific named disease such as cancer. Benefits can be paid as expenses incurred, per diem, or as a lump sum.

A risk with the possibility of gain, loss, or no change — such as investing in a business or the stock market. Generally NOT insurable. Contrasts with pure risk, which has only loss or no loss and IS insurable.
Coverage for loss of perishable stock due to power outage, refrigeration breakdown, or contamination. Critical for grocery, restaurants, pharmaceutical, and temperature-sensitive goods.
Coverage for property damage caused by accidental, untimely discharge from an automatic sprinkler system.

An automatic fire suppression system. Types include wet pipe (water always in pipes), dry pipe (air in pipes until a head activates), pre-action (two-stage activation), and deluge (all heads open at once). Properly maintained sprinklers are one of the most effective property loss controls.
Employee of the insurance company's claim department.

A credit rating assigned to insurance companies reflecting their ability to pay claims. Ranges from AAA (highest) down to D (default). Commonly required alongside or in place of AM Best ratings for certain accounts.

A person or property that, according to a company's underwriting standards, is considered a normal risk and insurable at standard rates. Better-than-standard risks may qualify for discounts; worse-than-standard may pay higher rates or be declined.
Trees that have not yet been cut. Property of the landowner; valued per board-foot or cord. Insurable under standing timber policies.
A specialty policy covering timber as it stands, typically against fire and lightning. Limits based on board-foot or cord valuation. Relevant for large landowners and timber investment accounts.
A piping system that delivers water to fire hoses within a building. Class I (fire department use), Class II (building occupant use), Class III (both).

A state-mandated pool funded by assessments on admitted insurers that pays claims to policyholders if an admitted insurer becomes insolvent. Coverage limits vary by state and line of business. One key reason admitted coverage provides extra consumer protection.

The state-level agency that regulates insurance companies, agents, and brokers doing business in that state. Issues licenses, approves rates and forms, monitors solvency, and handles consumer complaints. Every state has one — often called the Department of Insurance (DOI).

The accounting rules insurance companies must use for financial statements filed with state regulators. More conservative than GAAP — prioritizes solvency monitoring over profit measurement. Requires certain assets to be "non-admitted" (excluded from the balance sheet).
A policy designed to protect a self-insured organization from the risk of a single catastrophic loss or cumulative losses above a predetermined attachment point.

The category of accidents involving a person being hit by a moving object — falling trees and limbs ("widowmakers"), rolling logs, swinging cables, falling loads. The leading cause of logging fatalities.
A complete package sent to an underwriter by a producer seeking a quote — applications, loss runs, financials, supplemental questionnaires, schedules, and narrative information about the risk.
The legal process by which an insurance company, after paying a loss, seeks to recover the amount of the loss from another party who is legally liable.

A risk that presents a higher-than-average chance of loss due to medical condition, hazardous occupation, or other factors. Requires the use of a waiver, a special policy form, or a higher premium charge.
A documented plan for managing personnel loss — replacing key people due to retirement, disability, death, or resignation — so the organization can continue operating.

Payments made IN ADDITION to the policy's limit of liability. Includes: cost of bail bonds up to $2,000 for violations because of a covered accident; expenses the insured incurs at the insurer's request, including lost earnings up to $250 a day; court costs the insured is required to pay because of a suit; interest that accrues after a judgment and before it is paid; all expenses incurred by the insurer.
An extra charge applied by the insurer — commonly for accidents, moving violations, or other adverse factors in auto insurance.

An arrangement where one party becomes answerable to a third party for the acts of a second party. Customarily an insurance company, the surety holds itself responsible to the obligee for the acts of the principal.

A specially licensed broker authorized to place business with non-admitted carriers when coverage is unavailable in the admitted market. Required to document the diligent effort to first seek admitted coverage.

Specialized property or liability coverage placed with an insurer not licensed in the state, used when coverage is unavailable from any licensed (admitted) insurer. Often used for unusual or high-risk exposures.

To terminate or cancel a life insurance policy before the maturity date. For a cash-value policy, the policyholder receives the cash surrender value (less any outstanding loans and surrender charges).
Broadest form. Any auto the insured will use during the policy period, including autos that are owned, leased, hired, rented, or borrowed.

term: Symbol 19 (Mobile Equipment Subject to Motor Vehicle Laws Only)
definition: Autos that would be considered mobile equipment if they were not subject to compulsory or financial responsibility laws or other motor vehicle laws.
Any auto the insured owns.
Any private passenger auto the insured owns.
Customers' autos left with the insured for service, repair, storage, or safekeeping (called garage keepers coverage).
Physical damage coverage only for the dealers' autos, including owned autos, autos for sale, and autos on consignment.

term: Symbol 4 (Owned Autos Other Than Private Passenger Autos Only)
definition: Other types of vehicles the insured owns, such as trucks, trailers, buses, and motorcycles.
Trailers borrowed or leased by the named insured for which liability for loss has been assumed under a written trailer interchange agreement.
Trailers owned or hired by the named insured while the trailers are in someone else's possession under a written trailer interchange agreement.

term: Symbol 5 (Owned Autos Subject to No-Fault Law)
definition: Designates owned autos required to have no-fault benefits in a particular state.

term: Symbol 6 (Owned Autos Subject to Compulsory UM Law)
definition: Designates owned autos required to have uninsured motorists coverage in a particular state.
Most restrictive form. Applies only to autos specifically listed in the business auto coverage form declarations.
Only used for autos the insured has leased, hired, rented, or borrowed. Does not include autos rented or borrowed from employees.
Only used for autos used in the insured's business that are not leased, hired, rented, or borrowed. Includes autos owned by employees but used in the insured's business or personal affairs.
A discussion-based drill where managers walk through an incident scenario to test plans and decision-making, without physical deployment. Lower cost and complexity than full-scale exercises.
Property with physical existence. Includes real property (land, buildings, fixed improvements) and personal property (movable property such as equipment, inventory, vehicles).

Electronic devices or smartphone apps that capture vehicle data — location, speed, hard braking, acceleration, cornering, idle time. Used in fleet safety programs to coach drivers and in commercial auto underwriting to price policies based on actual behavior.
Life insurance for which premiums are paid over a limited time and that covers a specific term; the face value is payable only if death occurs within the term.

A person other than the insured or insurer who has incurred losses, or who is entitled to receive payment due to acts or omissions of the insured. A third-party claim is made against someone else's insurance.
A situation involving a person other than the insurer and insured; i.e., a person making a liability claim against the insured.

A governance model for risk management: (1) operational management owns and manages risk; (2) risk and compliance functions provide oversight; (3) internal audit provides independent assurance. Widely adopted in financial services.
The chain-and-lever device used to secure logs to a truck. Improper tensioning or release causes many injuries. Standing to the side of released binders is the rule.
A category of coverages triggered by the time it takes to recover from a loss — business income, extra expense, extended period of indemnity, civil authority, and ingress/egress.

Coverage that guarantees the validity of a title to real property (and sometimes personal property). Protects buyers and mortgage lenders against losses from undiscovered defects — liens, encumbrances, or ownership disputes — that existed before the policy was issued. Two types: Lender's Policy and Owner's Policy.
When you take out a mortgage, the lender requires lender's title insurance to protect the lender's investment against losses from title claims. This policy protects the lender only — not the buyer.
Separate title insurance policy that protects the buyer's financial interest in the property. Strongly recommended because the lender's policy does not protect the owner.

The full cost of risk to an organization: insurance premiums + retained losses + loss control expenses + risk management administration + indirect costs (lost productivity, reputation damage). TCOR analysis is the foundation of strategic risk management.
The rate of all OSHA-recordable incidents per 100 full-time workers per year. Formula: (Recordable incidents × 200,000) ÷ total hours worked. Key benchmark for comparing workplace safety performance.

Covers damage to a specific trailer under the policy of the trucker in whose possession the trailer is at the time of loss. For coverage to apply, the trucker must be liable under a written interchange agreement, and the damage must be caused by a covered peril.

Covers financial loss due to trip cancellation or interruption; lost or damaged baggage; trip or baggage delays; missed connections or itinerary changes; and losses due to rental vehicle damage or medical emergencies while traveling.
A reinsurance agreement between the ceding company and reinsurer in which the reinsurer automatically accepts a share of all risks that meet specified criteria within the treaty.

A modified version of the business auto coverage form that takes into consideration the special practices and regulations that apply to the trucking industry. Includes three coverages: (1) liability, (2) trailer interchange, (3) physical damage.

term: Two Main Processes of Risk Management
definition: (1) Decision process — identifying, analyzing, and selecting risk management techniques; (2) Administrative process — implementing, monitoring, and adjusting those techniques over time.
Property (real and personal), personal (bodily injury, death, disability), net income (lost revenue, extra expense), and liability (third-party claims).
Natural perils (weather, flood, earthquake), human perils (theft, vandalism, error), and economic perils (recession, currency fluctuation, supplier failure).

Non-business liability protection for individuals above a specific limit set in a basic policy (auto, homeowners). Also covers known or unknown gaps in basic coverages. Typically starts at $1 million.
For Property coverage, if a company and a claimant fail to agree on the amount of loss, each may appoint an appraiser, and these in turn select an umpire. A decision by two of the three is binding.
An employee of an insurance company who evaluates an individual's risk factors and assigns premium amounts.
The process an insurance company uses to decide whether to accept or reject an application for a policy.
Amount of premium for which payment has been made by the policyholder but coverage has not yet been provided. If a policy is canceled, the unearned premium is refunded (pro-rata or short-rate).
Pays you and your passengers for bodily injury caused by a negligent uninsured motorist, a hit-and-run driver, or by a driver whose insurer is insolvent.
Pays for damage to your automobile, up to a stated limit, when caused by a negligent uninsured motorist.
Auto insurance that protects you against another driver not having enough coverage to pay the entirety of your losses.
Adjustable life insurance under which premiums and coverage amounts can be flexibly changed. Includes a cash-value component that earns interest, with mortality and expense charges deducted monthly.
A risk we mistakenly think we understand but actually do not. Often more dangerous than unknown unknowns because organizations act with false confidence.
A risk that is not even recognized to exist — a "blindsiding" event. Cannot be planned for directly; mitigated through flexibility, resilience, and contingency funds.
An opportunity that arises out of uncertainty about outcomes. Not all risk is bad — some uncertainty creates upside potential (e.g., a new market opportunity).
The medical certification required for interstate commercial drivers, issued by a medical examiner on the FMCSA's National Registry. Valid for up to 24 months.
Coverage for loss of business income or property damage resulting from a loss at a utility — power plant, water plant, communication facility — that serves the insured.
The legal principle that both parties to an insurance contract owe each other complete honesty and full disclosure. More demanding than the standard of good faith in ordinary commercial contracts.

An insurance contract for which the value is agreed upon in advance and is paid in full in the event of a total loss, regardless of the actual cash value at the time of loss. Common for fine art, antiques, collectibles.

Life insurance whose face value and cash value vary based on the performance of underlying investments (stocks, bonds, mutual funds) chosen by the policyholder. Higher potential reward and higher risk than whole or universal life.
Combines the flexible premium features of universal life with the investment component of variable life. Cash value depends on investment performance of separate accounts the policyholder selects.
Limited-benefit expense policy providing benefits for eye care — routine eye exams, glasses, and contact lenses. May include surgical benefits for eye injury or illness.
The degree of probability that a loss will occur. Higher vulnerability means a given peril is more likely to result in loss.
A period of time set forth in a policy that must pass before some or all coverages begin. Common in disability, long-term care, and health insurance policies.

A contract or policy provision in which an insurer agrees not to pursue recovery from a specific third party, even after paying a claim. Often required by lease and contract terms to prevent insurance disputes between business partners.

A statement by the insured that certain facts are or will be true. Historically, any breach of warranty voided the contract; modern law in many states requires materiality. A "protective safeguard warranty" requires the insured to maintain specified safety systems.
Life insurance with premiums paid throughout the lifetime of the insured.

A broker who places business for retail agents, often accessing surplus lines markets or specialty carriers not available to retail. Retail agent brings the relationship and risk; wholesaler brings market access.
A dead or broken branch, or a partially fallen tree, that can fall unexpectedly. A major hazard in logging operations. Inspection and removal before felling and yarding is a core control.

State-mandated insurance that provides coverage for employee injuries and illnesses that occur on the job. Pays medical expenses and lost wages for injured workers and protects employers from most employee injury lawsuits.

The psychological cost of bearing uncertainty. Increases with: higher variability of possible losses; lower decision-maker confidence; larger possible loss size; higher probability of maximum loss; weaker financial strength; less insurance coverage. Insurance reduces worry value even when actual expected losses are unchanged.

The total premium contractually charged by the insurer to the policyholder for the effective period of the contract. Differs from earned premium — written premium is contractually owed; earned premium is what the insurer has "earned" through time in force.
Moving felled and bucked logs from the stump to a landing area where they can be loaded. Ground-based (skidder, forwarder), cable (yarder), or aerial (helicopter) yarding systems.
